Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n\nDoes charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n\n- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.206159Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.203379Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.224003Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.222997Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.226201Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.226201Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:58.774707Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:58.774707Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "

We convert each band to its midpoint as a numeric estimate (`owners_est`), but keep in mind every game inside the same band is tied." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 2, "id": "d8e51c69", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.061231Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.061231Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.081926Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.081926Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Distinct owner bands: 13\n", "\n", "Games per band:\n", "owners\n", "0-20000 18596\n", "100000-200000 1386\n", "1000000-2000000 288\n", "10000000-20000000 21\n", "100000000-200000000 1\n", "20000-50000 3059\n", "200000-500000 1272\n", "2000000-5000000 193\n", "20000000-50000000 3\n", "50000-100000 1695\n", "500000-1000000 513\n", "5000000-10000000 46\n", "50000000-100000000 2\n", "Name: count, dtype: int64\n" ] } ], "source": [ "def band_to_midpoint(s):\n", " lo, hi = s.split('-')\n", " return (int(lo) + int(hi)) / 2\n", "\n", "df['owners_est'] = df['owners'].apply(band_to_midpoint)\n", "\n", "# How coarse is the metric? "c2f61b11", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 4. Problem 2 — does \"number of selling\" identify the same games as quality?\n", "\n", "If sales and quality point to the same winners, sales is a defensible single definition of success. If they disagree a lot, then \"number of selling\" captures only one face of success. We measure agreement two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Spearman rank correlation** over all games with both metrics.\n", "- **Top-N overlap (Jaccard)** between the top games by each definition." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 6, "id": "bf7affb0", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.156195Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.155198Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.171161Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.170653Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Spearman(owners, quality) = 0.028 (n=3824)\n", "Top-20: 1/20 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-50: 3/50 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-100: 7/100 games shared, Jaccard=0.04\n" ] } ], "source": [ "both = df[df['quality'].notna()].copy()\n", "# Spearman = Pearson on ranks (avoids a scipy dependency).\n", "rho = both['owners_est'].rank().corr(both['quality'].rank())\n", "print('Spearman(owners, quality) = %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho, len(both)))\n", "\n", "def topn_jaccard(frame, col_a, col_b, n=100):\n", " a = set(frame.sort_values(col_a, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " b = set(frame.sort_values(col_b, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " return len(a & b) / len(a | b), len(a & b)\n", "\n", "for n in (20, 50, 100):\n", " j, overlap = topn_jaccard(both, 'owners_est', 'quality', n)\n", " print('Top-%d: %d/%d games shared, Jaccard=%.2f' % (n, overlap, n, j))" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 7, "id": "3ce8ead7", "metadata": { "execution": { Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.186220Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.186220Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.203379Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.203379Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n\nDoes charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n\n- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.206159Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.203379Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.224003Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:07:57.222997Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? aiTitle: Analyze success definitions by game sales metrics

content.text:
This confirms your point precisely. Look at the top of the playtime distribution:

- **`average_playtime` is wildly skewed:** median 4.2h but mean 14.7h, 99th percentile 178h, **max 1,587h**.
- **The extreme-playtime games are mostly niche, not famous:** *The Banner Saga: Factions* (1,587h, only 200k–500k owners, 771 ratings), *Boundless* (920h, 20k–50k owners), *Shroud of the Avatar* (910h, 50k–100k owners, and a poor 0.51 rating). These are small-audience sandbox/MMO grinders, exactly the case you described.

Two mitigating facts and one real problem:
- **Spearman is rank-based**, so the *correlation numbers* I reported are already robust to these magnitude outliers (winsorizing wouldn't change them) — the "weak relationship" conclusion holds.
- **But the band means are not safe:** the `50h+` band is populated by these niche games, so its mean reception can be pulled around by a handful of titles, and nothing controlled for "famous."

Let me run the robustness checks that actually matter here.

input.command:
cd "D:\Study\Lab\human-AI-logs" && python -c "
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
df=pd.read_csv('data/steam.csv')
df['total']=df['positive_ratings']+df['negative_ratings']
df['pos_ratio']=df['positive_ratings']/df['total'].replace(0,np.nan)
q=df[df['total']>=500].copy()
q=q[q['average_playtime']>0].copy()
q['is_free']=q['price']==0
q['owners_lo']=q['owners'].str.split('-').str[0].astype(float)

def sp(a,b):
    m=a.notna()&b.notna(); return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())

print('== Robustness 1: median_playtime (robust to per-game player outliers) ==')
for lab,sub in [('FREE',q[q['is_free']]),('PAID',q[~q['is_free']])]:
    r,n=sp(sub['median_playtime'],sub['pos_ratio']); print('%-4s Spearman(median_pt,pos)=%+.3f n=%d'%(lab,r,n))

print('\n== Robustness 2: famous games only (owners lower-bound >= 200k) ==')
fam=q[q['owners_lo']>=200000]
print('famous n=%d (%.0f%% of sample)'%(len(fam),100*len(fam)/len(q)))
for lab,sub in [('FREE',fam[fam['is_free']]),('PAID',fam[~fam['is_free']])]:
    r,n=sp(sub['average_playtime'],sub['pos_ratio']); print('%-4s Spearman(avg_pt,pos)=%+.3f n=%d'%(lab,r,n))

print('\n== Robustness 3: band table with MEDIAN pos_ratio + counts (paid) ==')
q['hrs']=q['average_playtime']/60
bins=[0,1,2,5,10,20,50,1e9]; labels=['<1h','1-2h','2-5h','5-10h','10-20h','20-50h','50h+']
q['pb']=pd.cut(q['hrs'],bins=bins,labels=labels,right=False)
paid=q[~q['is_free']]
t=paid.groupby('pb',observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count','mean','median']).round(3)
print(t)
print('\nShare of paid sample in the fragile 50h+ band: %.1f%%'%(100*(paid['pb']=='50h+').mean()))
"

content.text:
These robustness checks genuinely change the story — your critique was well-founded:

| Check | Free | Paid | Takeaway |
|---|---|---|---|
| Original (avg playtime, full sample) | 0.00 | +0.064 | mild paid-only signal |
| **Median** playtime (robust) | −0.03 | +0.03 | signal nearly **vanishes** — the avg-based positive was partly the skewed tail |
| **Famous only** (owners ≥ 200k) | **+0.106** | **+0.113** | signal **strengthens** once niche grinders are removed — and free is *not* flat anymore |

Robust band table (paid) confirms the 5–20h peak survives using the **median** positive ratio (0.861), so it isn't a few odd games — but the `50h+` band is only **4.2%** of paid games, so it's fragile.

So: removing the niche long-playtime outliers (your point) actually *reveals* a cleaner modest positive relationship, and my earlier "free is flat" conclusion was an artifact of niche free titles. Let me add this as a robustness subsection under Question 3 and fix the verdict.

content.text: Confirmed. I'll insert the robustness markdown + code between the plot (`4f24ccc0`) and the verdict, then update the verdict.

input.new_source:
# --- Robustness for Question 3: skew, outliers, and "famous" ---
# average_playtime is highly right-skewed -> a few niche games dominate the tail.
print('avg playtime hours -> median=%.1f  mean=%.1f  99th=%.1f  max=%.1f'
      % (pt['hours'].median(), pt['hours'].mean(), pt['hours'].quantile(0.99), pt['hours'].max()))
pt['owners_lo'] = pt['owners'].str.split('-').str[0].astype(float)
print('\nLongest-playtime games are mostly niche (small audience):')
print(pt.sort_values('hours', ascending=False).head(5)[['name','hours','owners','total_ratings','pos_ratio']]
        .assign(hours=lambda d: d['hours'].round(0)).to_string(index=False))

# Check 1 - MEDIAN playtime instead of the mean (robust to per-game player outliers)
print('\n[1] Spearman with MEDIAN playtime (robust to skew):')
for lab, sub in [('FREE', pt[pt['is_free']]), ('PAID', pt[~pt['is_free']])]:
    rho, n = spearman(sub['median_playtime'], sub['pos_ratio'])
    print('    %-4s = %+.3f (n=%d)' % (lab, rho, n))

# Check 2 - restrict to "famous" games (owners lower-bound >= 200k) to drop the niche tail
fam = pt[pt['owners_lo'] >= 200000]
print('\n[2] FAMOUS games only (owners >= 200k, n=%d):' % len(fam))
for lab, sub in [('FREE', fam[fam['is_free']]), ('PAID', fam[~fam['is_free']])]:
    rho, n = spearman(sub['average_playtime'], sub['pos_ratio'])
    print('    %-4s Spearman(avg_pt, pos) = %+.3f (n=%d)' % (lab, rho, n))

# Check 3 - band reception via the MEDIAN (resistant to a few odd games per band)
share_top = 100 * (pt[~pt['is_free']]['play_band'] == '50h+').mean()
print('\n[3] Paid band reception, mean vs median (50h+ band is only %.1f%% of paid):' % share_top)
print(pt[~pt['is_free']].groupby('play_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio']
        .agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3))

Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.674419Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.673897Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.677170Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.675424Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.692627Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "

aiTitle: Analyze success definitions by game sales metrics

input.new_source:
## Robustness — outliers, skew, and "famous"

`average_playtime` is a **mean**, so it is right-skewed and a few niche games with enormous per-player time (e.g. *The Banner Saga: Factions*, 1,587h on a small audience) sit far out in the tail. Three checks below test whether the conclusion survives:

1. **Median playtime** instead of the mean — robust to per-game player outliers.
2. **Famous games only** (`owners` lower-bound ≥ 200k) — drops the niche, small-audience grinders the user flagged.
3. **Median positive ratio per band** — so a band's reception isn't swung by a handful of odd titles.

(The Spearman correlations themselves are rank-based, so they are already immune to the *magnitude* of these outliers — these checks probe whether the *signal* is real, not just whether outliers inflate a number.)

Problem 2 — does \"number of selling\" identify the same games as quality?\n", "\n", "If sales and quality point to the same winners, sales is a defensible single definition of success. If they disagree a lot, then \"number of selling\" captures only one face of success. We measure agreement two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Spearman rank correlation** over all games with both metrics.\n", "- **Top-N overlap (Jaccard)** between the top games by each definition." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 6, "id": "bf7affb0", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.628256Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.628256Z", quality) = 0.028 (n=3824)\n", "Top-20: 1/20 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-50: 3/50 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-100: 7/100 games shared, Jaccard=0.04\n" ] } ], "source": [ "both = df[df['quality'].notna()].copy()\n", "# Spearman = Pearson on ranks (avoids a scipy dependency).\n", "rho = both['owners_est'].rank().corr(both['quality'].rank())\n", "print('Spearman(owners, quality) = %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho, len(both)))\n", "\n", "def topn_jaccard(frame, col_a, col_b, n=100):\n", " a = set(frame.sort_values(col_a, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " b = set(frame.sort_values(col_b, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " return len(a & b) / len(a | b), len(a & b)\n", "\n", "for n in (20, 50, 100):\n", " j, overlap = topn_jaccard(both, 'owners_est', 'quality', n)\n", " print('Top-%d: %d/%d games shared, Jaccard=%.2f' % (n, overlap, n, j))" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 7, "id": "3ce8ead7", "metadata": { "execution": { Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.674419Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.673897Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.677170Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.675424Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.692627Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "

Build Definition A — \"number of selling\"\n", "\n", "`owners` is **not an exact count** — it is a *band* such as `10000000-20000000`. That is the first warning sign for using it as a precise success score. We convert each band to its midpoint as a numeric estimate (`owners_est`), but keep in mind every game inside the same band is tied." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 2, "id": "d8e51c69", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.530188Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.530188Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.552721Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.552721Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Distinct owner bands: 13\n", "\n", "Games per band:\n", "owners\n", "0-20000 18596\n", "100000-200000 1386\n", "1000000-2000000 288\n", "10000000-20000000 21\n", "100000000-200000000 1\n", "20000-50000 3059\n", "200000-500000 1272\n", "2000000-5000000 193\n", "20000000-50000000 3\n", "50000-100000 1695\n", "500000-1000000 513\n", "5000000-10000000 46\n", "50000000-100000000 2\n", "Name: count, dtype: int64\n" ] } ], "source": [ "def band_to_midpoint(s):\n", " lo, hi = s.split('-')\n", " return (int(lo) + int(hi)) / 2\n", "\n", "df['owners_est'] = df['owners'].apply(band_to_midpoint)\n", "\n", "# How coarse is the metric? "c2f61b11", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 4. Problem 2 — does \"number of selling\" identify the same games as quality?\n", "\n", "If sales and quality point to the same winners, sales is a defensible single definition of success. If they disagree a lot, then \"number of selling\" captures only one face of success. We measure agreement two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Spearman rank correlation** over all games with both metrics.\n", "- **Top-N overlap (Jaccard)** between the top games by each definition." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 6, "id": "bf7affb0", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.628256Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.628256Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.643221Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.643221Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Spearman(owners, quality) = 0.028 (n=3824)\n", "Top-20: 1/20 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-50: 3/50 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-100: 7/100 games shared, Jaccard=0.04\n" ] } ], "source": [ "both = df[df['quality'].notna()].copy()\n", "# Spearman = Pearson on ranks (avoids a scipy dependency).\n", "rho = both['owners_est'].rank().corr(both['quality'].rank())\n", "print('Spearman(owners, quality) = %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho, len(both)))\n", "\n", "def topn_jaccard(frame, col_a, col_b, n=100):\n", " a = set(frame.sort_values(col_a, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " b = set(frame.sort_values(col_b, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " return len(a & b) / len(a | b), len(a & b)\n", "\n", "for n in (20, 50, 100):\n", " j, overlap = topn_jaccard(both, 'owners_est', 'quality', n)\n", " print('Top-%d: %d/%d games shared, Jaccard=%.2f' % (n, overlap, n, j))" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 7, "id": "3ce8ead7", "metadata": { "execution": { Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.656409Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.674419Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.673897Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.677170Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.675424Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.692627Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:59.203084Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "

lastPrompt: seems like you didn't consider outliners or other things like that when doing the analysis? for example some games have very long avg play time, but that game actually not that famous

aiTitle: Analyze success definitions by game sales metrics

input.new_source:
## Verdict — playtime vs reception (robustness-checked)

**The relationship is weak, and *how* weak depends on the playtime measure and on whether you control for popularity — so the honest answer is nuanced.**

- **Rank-based, so magnitude outliers don't distort the correlations.** Spearman uses ranks, so a game like *The Banner Saga: Factions* (1,587h on a small audience) cannot inflate the numbers.
- **With the robust median playtime the signal nearly vanishes** (Free −0.03, Paid +0.03): the mild positive on *average* playtime was partly an artifact of the skewed tail.
- **But among *famous* games (owners ≥ 200k) the signal strengthens to ≈ +0.11 for both free and paid.** Stripping out the niche, small-audience grinders reveals a clearer (still modest) "played more → rated a bit higher" effect — and **free is no longer flat** once popularity is controlled. The earlier "free is flat" result was driven by niche free titles.
- **The 5–20h band peak survives** using the *median* positive ratio per band (≈ 0.86), so it isn't a few odd games; but the `50h+` band is only ~4% of paid titles and should be read cautiously.

**Bottom line:** playtime is a *mild* positive signal of reception that is easy to over- or under-state. Reported on raw averages across the whole catalogue it looks like a paid-only effect; measured robustly (median) it looks like noise; and on a popularity-restricted sample a modest positive effect appears for **both** free and paid. The split by free/paid still matters, but "famous vs niche" matters just as much.

Problem 2 — does \"number of selling\" identify the same games as quality?\n", "\n", "If sales and quality point to the same winners, sales is a defensible single definition of success. If they disagree a lot, then \"number of selling\" captures only one face of success. = %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho, len(both)))\n", "\n", "def topn_jaccard(frame, col_a, col_b, n=100):\n", " a = set(frame.sort_values(col_a, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " b = set(frame.sort_values(col_b, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " return len(a & b) / len(a | b), len(a & b)\n", "\n", "for n in (20, 50, 100):\n", " j, overlap = topn_jaccard(both, 'owners_est', Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.677170Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.675424Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.693233Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:27:58.692627Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. content.text:
The robustness section is in and executed. You were right to push on this — the outlier handling materially refined the conclusion.

## What I added (under Question 3, before its verdict)

A **"Robustness — outliers, skew, and 'famous'"** subsection with three checks, plus an updated, robustness-checked verdict. Embedded outputs:

- **Skew is severe:** avg playtime median 4.2h vs mean 14.7h, max 1,587h. The longest-playtime games are niche (*Banner Saga: Factions* — 1,587h on only 200k–500k owners; *Shroud of the Avatar* — 910h, 0.51 rating). Exactly your example.
- **[1] Median playtime (robust):** signal nearly vanishes — Free −0.03, Paid +0.03.
- **[2] Famous games only (owners ≥ 200k):** signal *strengthens* to **+0.11 for both** free and paid — and free is no longer flat once the niche tail is removed.
- **[3] Robust band table:** the 5–20h reception peak survives using the median (≈0.86), but the `50h+` band is just 4.2% of paid games, so it's fragile.

## The honest takeaway now

Playtime is a *mild* positive signal of reception that's easy to mis-state: raw averages → "paid-only effect"; robust median → "noise"; popularity-restricted → "modest positive for both." So **"famous vs niche" matters as much as "free vs paid."**

Two caveats worth noting on the method:
- Spearman is rank-based, so the *correlation numbers* were never inflated by the magnitude of those 1,587h outliers — but they did distort the *band means*, which is what the robustness checks fix.
- I haven't applied the same outlier scrutiny retroactively to Questions 1–2 (e.g. the `price` analysis also has a long tail at $40+ with few titles). Want me to add a brief robustness note there too, or leave those as-is?

The file is now 141 KB (third plot/section embedded) — reload it in your IDE to view. Let me know if you'd like the famous-vs-niche split shown as its own plot.

content: You're analyzing Steam data in notebook.ipynb, answering three questions about game success, price, and playtime versus reception. Question 3 now has outlier robustness checks added and executed. Next: reload the notebook to view results, or extend robustness checks to Questions 1-2.

message.content: For question 1, you mentioned reception quality(you analysis this, seems reasonable), but what is "revenue"?

content.text:
Good question — "revenue" there is just a **rough proxy I computed**, not a column in the dataset. It appears in Question 1, Section 3 (the cell that starts `# A truer 'number of selling'...`):

```python
df['revenue_est'] = df['owners_est'] * df['price']   # rough revenue proxy
```

So it's literally:

**revenue_est = owners_est × price**

where:
- **`owners_est`** = the midpoint of the `owners` band (e.g. `10000000-20000000` → 15,000,000) — an *estimate of units acquired*.
- **`price`** = the game's current listed price.

The idea: `owners` alone counts *downloads* (and 42% of the top "sellers" are free, so they earned $0). Multiplying estimated units by price gives a crude estimate of *money taken in* — a "truer number of selling" than raw owner count. That's why the revenue ranking (Witcher 3, Monster Hunter, GTA V…) looks very different from the raw-owners ranking (which is topped by free games like Dota 2).

**Important caveats — it is a weak proxy, not real revenue:**
1. **Current price, not sale price.** Most copies sell during discounts/bundles; `price` is today's sticker price, so it overstates per-unit take.
2. **Owner bands are coarse.** `owners_est` is a band midpoint, so every game in a band shares the same unit estimate — large rounding error, multiplied by price.
3. **No Steam cut / refunds / regional pricing / taxes.** Valve takes ~30%, prices vary by region, refunds happen — none captured.
4. **Free-to-play revenue is invisible.** Dota 2 / CS:GO earn huge sums via in-game purchases, but `price = 0` zeroes them out, so this proxy *understates* F2P games.

So `revenue_est` was used only as an illustration in Section 3 — to show that "number of selling" reorders completely once you weight by price — **not** as one of the two formal definitions of success (those are A = sales/owners, B = reception quality).

Want me to:
1. **Add a short clarifying note** in that Section 3 cell/markdown spelling out the `revenue_est = owners × price` formula and these caveats, so the notebook is self-explanatory, or
2. Leave it and just keep this explanation here?

message.content: the definition of renvenue makes sense but the formulate is wired so I'd like not take this. Is there any other features that can represent that?

content.text:
Short answer: **no — this dataset has no revenue/sales‑dollar feature, and nothing else can stand in for it on its own.** The 18 columns are:

`appid, name, release_date, english, developer, publisher, platforms, required_age, categories, genres, steamspy_tags, achievements, positive_ratings, negative_ratings, average_playtime, median_playtime, owners, price`

The only two commercially‑flavored fields are **`owners`** (a units estimate) and **`price`**. Revenue is *by definition* units × price, so you can't get it from any single existing column — which is exactly why my proxy had to multiply them, and exactly why it came out "weird." If you drop the multiplication, revenue isn't recoverable here.

So you have two reasonable paths:

**Option 1 — Drop the revenue idea entirely.** Keep Question 1 clean with just the two definitions you liked:
- **A = "number of selling"** (`owners`)
- **B = reception quality** (`pos_ratio`)

This is the simplest and most honest given the data.

**Option 2 — Replace revenue with a *single‑feature* commercial proxy** that avoids the price multiplication. The standard one is **review count** = `positive_ratings + negative_ratings` (your existing `total_ratings`):
- Number of reviews scales with copies sold (the well‑known "Boxleiter" sales‑estimation method), so it's a cleaner, finer‑grained units proxy than the coarse `owners` bands — and it needs no price arithmetic.
- Caveat: it still measures *units/popularity*, not *dollars*. Free games with many players will look big, same as `owners`. It does **not** solve the "downloads ≠ selling" problem — only a price term does that, which you've chosen to avoid.

My recommendation: **Option 1** — remove `revenue_est` from Section 3, since neither the multiplication nor a single proxy genuinely captures dollar revenue, and the two definitions (sales vs reception) already answer the question.

Want me to:
1. **Remove the `revenue_est` line and its revenue ranking** from Section 3 (and trim the Section 6 verdict's mention of it), or
2. **Swap it for the review‑count proxy** as an alternative "units sold" view?

Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.971620Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.970604Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.987581Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.987581Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.989610Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.989610Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.006395Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.005776Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.008874Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.007867Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.505083Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.504571Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "

Build Definition A — \"number of selling\"\n", "\n", "`owners` is **not an exact count** — it is a *band* such as `10000000-20000000`. aiTitle: Analyze success definitions by game sales metrics

input.new_source:
## 6. Verdict — is "number of selling" a reasonable definition of success?

**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**

| Check | Result | Reading |
|---|---|---|
| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |
| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |
| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |
| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |

**Verdict: "number of selling" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data — but a perfectly *explainable* one.**

*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.

*Against:*
1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest "sellers" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies — `owners` counts everyone who *acquired* a game, rewarding heavily-downloaded free titles as if they were top sellers.
2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing "success = sales" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.
3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.

**Bottom line:** the two definitions — `owners` (sales) and reception quality — do **not** identify the same games, so "number of selling" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best read *alongside* the reception-quality definition, not on its own.

Problem 2 — does \"number of selling\" identify the same games as quality?\n", "\n", "If sales and quality point to the same winners, sales is a defensible single definition of success. If they disagree a lot, then \"number of selling\" captures only one face of success. We measure agreement two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Spearman rank correlation** over all games with both metrics.\n", "- **Top-N overlap (Jaccard)** between the top games by each definition." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 6, "id": "bf7affb0", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.934098Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.934098Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.953960Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.953960Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Spearman(owners, quality) = 0.028 (n=3824)\n", "Top-20: 1/20 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-50: 3/50 games shared, Jaccard=0.03\n", "Top-100: 7/100 games shared, Jaccard=0.04\n" ] } ], "source": [ "both = df[df['quality'].notna()].copy()\n", "# Spearman = Pearson on ranks (avoids a scipy dependency).\n", "rho = both['owners_est'].rank().corr(both['quality'].rank())\n", "print('Spearman(owners, quality) = %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho, len(both)))\n", "\n", "def topn_jaccard(frame, col_a, col_b, n=100):\n", " a = set(frame.sort_values(col_a, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " b = set(frame.sort_values(col_b, ascending=False).head(n)['appid'])\n", " return len(a & b) / len(a | b), len(a & b)\n", "\n", "for n in (20, 50, 100):\n", " j, overlap = topn_jaccard(both, 'owners_est', 'quality', n)\n", " print('Top-%d: %d/%d games shared, Jaccard=%.2f' % (n, overlap, n, j))" ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 7, "id": "3ce8ead7", "metadata": { "execution": { Problem 3 — the banding creates massive ties\n", "\n", "Because `owners` is a band, you cannot rank games *within* a band. The most successful band (`0-20000`) holds the majority of the catalogue, so for ~70% of games \"number of selling\" gives them an identical score and cannot distinguish a success from a failure at all." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 8, "id": "488b2b72", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.971620Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.970604Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.987581Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.987581Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): 68.7%\n", "\n", "Within a single owners band, quality ranges:\n", " count min max\n", "owners \n", "0-20000 56 0.310502 0.996176\n", "100000-200000 817 0.139937 0.979218\n", "1000000-2000000 286 0.243491 0.985136\n", "10000000-20000000 21 0.548671 0.986504\n", "100000000-200000000 1 0.858710 0.858710\n", "20000-50000 388 0.297521 0.986767\n", "200000-500000 991 0.134011 0.981645\n", "2000000-5000000 193 0.396119 0.975358\n", "20000000-50000000 3 0.902850 0.938107\n", "50000-100000 532 0.151163 0.987281\n", "500000-1000000 488 0.242442 0.982268\n", "5000000-10000000 46 0.426182 0.976902\n", "50000000-100000000 2 0.504632 0.867952\n" ] } ], "source": [ "share_in_lowest = (df['owners'] == '0-20000').mean()\n", "print('Share of catalogue stuck in the lowest band (tied): %.1f%%' % (100*share_in_lowest))\n", "\n", "# Within the top band, quality varies wildly -> sales alone cannot separate them.\n", "top_band = df[df['owners'] == df.sort_values('owners_est').iloc[-1]['owners']]\n", "print('\\nWithin a single owners band, quality ranges:')\n", "print(both.groupby('owners')['quality'].agg(['count','min','max']).sort_index())" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "974c7a75", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "## 6. Verdict — is \"number of selling\" a reasonable definition of success?\n", "\n", "**Observed on this dataset (n = 3,824 games with enough ratings):**\n", "\n", "| Check | Result | Reading |\n", "|---|---|---|\n", "| Free games in top-50 by `owners` | **42%** | `owners` largely measures downloads, not selling |\n", "| Spearman(owners, quality) | **≈ 0.03** | sales and reception are essentially **uncorrelated** |\n", "| Top-100 overlap (sales vs quality) | **7 / 100** (Jaccard 0.04) | the two definitions identify **almost entirely different** games |\n", "| Catalogue tied in lowest band | **~69%** | sales cannot rank most of the library at all |\n", "\n", "**Verdict: \"number of selling\" is a *weak and incomplete* definition of success on this data.**\n", "\n", "*In favour:* it is objective and commercially meaningful — a game in the 10M+ band is undeniably a hit in business terms.\n", "\n", "*Against:*\n", "1. **It measures downloads, not selling.** 42% of the biggest \"sellers\" are free (`price = 0`), so they sold zero copies. A revenue proxy (`owners x price`, Section 3) reorders the winners completely.\n", "2. **It barely agrees with quality.** Spearman ≈ 0.03 and only 7 of the top 100 games are shared with the quality ranking (Section 4). Choosing \"success = sales\" therefore discards the entire reception dimension — there are big sellers that are mediocre and beloved games that sold little.\n", "3. **It is too coarse to rank ~69% of titles** (Section 5), which sit tied in the bottom `0-20000` band.\n", "\n", "**Bottom line:** the two definitions do **not** identify the same games, so \"number of selling\" alone is not a reasonable stand-alone definition of success here. It is best used *together* with a second definition (revenue and/or reception quality), not on its own." ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "ec1f8db2", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "# Question 2 — How does price relate to player reception?\n", "\n", "Does charging more (or less) go together with better player reception? We use the same reception measure as Definition B (`pos_ratio`, guarded by `MIN_RATINGS`) and relate it to `price` two ways:\n", "\n", "- **Rank correlation** — Spearman(price, pos_ratio), for all games and for paid games only.\n", "- **Price bands** — average reception across free / cheap / mid / premium tiers, which can reveal a *non-linear* pattern a single correlation would hide." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 9, "id": "8fc16b00", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.989610Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:05.989610Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.006395Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.005776Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "name": "stdout", "output_type": "stream", "text": [ "Games analysed (>= 500 ratings): 3824\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : 0.053 (n=3824)\n", "Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: -0.052 (n=3210)\n", "Mean pos_ratio free: 0.749 | paid: 0.803\n", "\n", "Reception by price band:\n", " count mean median\n", "price_band \n", "Free 614 0.749 0.774\n", "<$5 755 0.787 0.833\n", "$5-10 849 0.819 0.855\n", "$10-20 1152 0.817 0.849\n", "$20-40 399 0.767 0.786\n", "$40+ 55 0.744 0.762\n" ] } ], "source": [ "# --- Section 7: does price relate to player reception? ---\n", "# Reuse the quality guard from Section 2 (only games with enough votes).\n", "rq = df[df['total_ratings'] >= MIN_RATINGS].copy()\n", "print('Games analysed (>= %d ratings): %d' % (MIN_RATINGS, len(rq)))\n", "\n", "def spearman(a, b):\n", " m = a.notna() & b.notna()\n", " return a[m].rank().corr(b[m].rank()), int(m.sum())\n", "\n", "rho_all, n_all = spearman(rq['price'], rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "paid_rq = rq[rq['price'] > 0]\n", "rho_paid, n_paid = spearman(paid_rq['price'], paid_rq['pos_ratio'])\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) ALL : %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_all, n_all))\n", "print('Spearman(price, pos_ratio) PAID: %.3f (n=%d)' % (rho_paid, n_paid))\n", "print('Mean pos_ratio free: %.3f | paid: %.3f' % (\n", " rq[rq['price'] == 0]['pos_ratio'].mean(), paid_rq['pos_ratio'].mean()))\n", "\n", "# Reception by price band (free, then ascending paid tiers)\n", "bins = [0, 0.01, 5, 10, 20, 40, 1e9]\n", "labels = ['Free', '<$5', '$5-10', '$10-20', '$20-40', '$40+']\n", "rq['price_band'] = pd.cut(rq['price'], bins=bins, labels=labels, right=False)\n", "band_tbl = rq.groupby('price_band', observed=True)['pos_ratio'].agg(['count', 'mean', 'median']).round(3)\n", "print('\\nReception by price band:')\n", "print(band_tbl)" ] }, { "cell_type": "markdown", "id": "852971e1", "metadata": {}, "source": [ "A single correlation number is near zero, so it is tempting to conclude \"price has no relation to reception.\" But binning by price band shows the relationship is **non-monotonic** — it rises then falls — which a rank correlation cannot capture. We visualise both the mean (the inverted-U) and the full distribution (the spread within each band) below." ] }, { "cell_type": "code", "execution_count": 10, "id": "04323878", "metadata": { "execution": { "iopub.execute_input": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.008874Z", "iopub.status.busy": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.007867Z", "iopub.status.idle": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.505083Z", "shell.execute_reply": "2026-06-08T02:35:06.504571Z" } }, "outputs": [ { "data": { "image/png": "", "text/plain": [ "
